Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Understand the Goal The user wants a DuckDB SQL query that filters out events from cases that contain a specific sequence of three activities Approve Purchase Requisition Create Order Confirm Order in direct succession where the time between Create Order and Confirm Order is more than 5 days The final result should include all events from cases that do not match these criteria

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Input Schema Table event_log Columns case_id activity timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Key Requirements Identify cases with the exact sequence Approve Purchase Requisition Create Order Confirm Order in direct succession no other events in between for the same case Among those filter to cases where timestamp Confirm Order minus timestamp Create Order is more than 5 days Exclude all events from these cases Return all other events from event_log

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Step by Step Logic Find cases that match the sequence condition For each case check if the time difference between Create Order and Confirm Order in that sequence is more than 5 days Collect the case ids that meet both conditions Select all rows from event_log where case_id is not in the collected list

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Handling Direct Succession In SQL we can use window functions like LEAD or LAG over partition by case_id order by timestamp to check the sequence Alternatively we can use self joins or MATCH RECOGNIZE Lets stick to window functions for clarity and reliability

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Actually DuckDB supports MATCH RECOGNIZE but window functions are usually preferred for simplicity Lets use LEAD

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Simpler approach Use LEAD activity 1 over partition by case_id order by timestamp and LEAD activity 2 over partition by case_id order by timestamp Filter where activity equals Approve Purchase Requisition and lead 1 equals Create Order and lead 2 equals Confirm Order This ensures direct succession

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: What if there are ties in timestamps The prompt says direct succession which typically implies ordering by timestamp I will assume order by timestamp is sufficient

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: To get the time difference we could join back to event_log or use window functions differently Alternative Use LEAD timestamp 1 and LEAD timestamp 2 as well

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: next timestamp is the timestamp of Create Order and next next timestamp is the timestamp of Confirm Order So next next timestamp minus next timestamp is exactly the time between them This works

Name: Validation - PE
Text: In DuckDB timestamp subtraction returns an interval and we can compare intervals directly next next timestamp minus next timestamp greater than interval 5 days should work

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Filtering Out Cases Now we have a list of case ids to exclude Final query select all from event_log where case_id is not in select case_id from sequenced

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Not in versus not exists versus left join is null Not in can have issues with nulls Not exists is safer I will use not exists for robustness

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The query uses a first common table expression to compute next activity next timestamp next next activity and next next timestamp for each event ordered within each case Then a second common table expression selects distinct case ids where the current activity is Approve Purchase Requisition the next is Create Order the next next is Confirm Order and the elapsed time between Create Order and Confirm Order is greater than 5 days Finally the main query returns all rows from event_log whose case_id does not exist in the excluded set ordered by case_id and timestamp

Name: Validation - PE
Text: LEAD window function supported Partition by and order by supported interval comparison supported common table expressions supported All good

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: If exact 24 hour periods are required interval comparison is more precise than datediff

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The prompt says direct succession and more than 5 days The query matches all requirements
